section.title: Before the Sanhedrin and the Plot on Paul
section.sectionRationale: Brought before the Sanhedrin, Paul is struck for claiming a clear conscience, then splits the council by naming the hope of resurrection that divides Pharisee from Sadducee. That night the Lord stands by him with the promise of Rome. A vow of forty conspirators to kill him is foiled by his nephew, and the commander sends him under heavy guard to Governor Felix in Caesarea.
section.thesis: Amid a hostile council, a murderous plot, and Roman politics, the risen Lord stands by Paul and pledges that he will testify in Rome, so that neither the Sanhedrin's rage nor forty sworn assassins can stop the word from reaching the heart of the empire.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the clash before the high priest; the council divided over the resurrection; the Lord's midnight promise of Rome; the plot of the forty and the nephew's warning; and the night march to Felix at Caesarea.

units.label: Before the Sanhedrin
units.note: a clear conscience, a struck mouth

verses.text: Paul looked directly at the {{synedrion|Sanhedrin}} and said, “Brothers, I have conducted myself before God in all good {{syneidesis|conscience}} to this day.”
At this, the high priest Ananias ordered those standing near Paul to strike him on the mouth.
Then Paul said to him, “God will strike you, you whitewashed wall! You sit here to judge me according to the law, yet you yourself violate the law by commanding that I be struck.”
But those standing nearby said, “How dare you insult the high priest of God!”
“Brothers,” Paul replied, “I was not aware that he was the high priest, for it is written: ‘Do not speak evil about the ruler of your people.’”

units.label: The council divided over the resurrection

verses.text: Then Paul, knowing that some of them were Sadducees and the others Pharisees, called out in the Sanhedrin, “Brothers, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee. It is because of my hope in the {{anastasis|resurrection}} of the dead that I am on trial.”
As soon as he had said this, a dispute broke out between the Pharisees and Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.
For the Sadducees say that there is neither a resurrection nor angels nor spirits, but the Pharisees acknowledge them all.
A great clamor arose, and some scribes from the party of the Pharisees got up and contended sharply, “We find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?”
The dispute grew so violent that the commander was afraid they would tear Paul to pieces. He ordered the soldiers to go down and remove him by force and bring him into the barracks.

units.label: The Lord's midnight promise of Rome
units.note: take courage, so you must testify in Rome

verses.text: The following night the Lord stood near Paul and said, “Take courage! As you have {{martyreo|testified}} about Me in Jerusalem, so also you must testify in Rome.”

units.label: The plot of the forty and the nephew's warning
units.note: an oath to kill, undone by a boy

verses.text: When daylight came, the Jews formed a conspiracy and bound themselves with an oath not to eat or drink until they had killed Paul.
More than forty of them were involved in this plot.
They went to the chief priests and elders and said, “We have bound ourselves with a solemn oath not to eat anything until we have killed Paul.
Now then, you and the Sanhedrin petition the commander to bring him down to you on the pretext of examining his case more carefully. We are ready to kill him on the way.”
But when the son of Paul’s sister heard about the plot, he went into the barracks and told Paul.
Then Paul called one of the centurions and said, “Take this young man to the commander; he has something to tell him.”
So the centurion took him to the commander and said, “Paul the prisoner sent and asked me to bring this young man to you. He has something to tell you.”
The commander took the young man by the hand, drew him aside, and asked, “What do you need to tell me?”
He answered, “The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul to the Sanhedrin tomorrow on the pretext of acquiring more information about him.

Do not let them persuade you, because more than forty men are waiting to ambush him. They have bound themselves with an oath not to eat or drink until they have killed him; they are ready now, awaiting your consent.”
So the commander dismissed the young man and instructed him, “Do not tell anyone that you have reported this to me.”
Then he called two of his centurions and said, “Prepare two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen to go to Caesarea in the third hour of the night.
Provide mounts for Paul to take him safely to Governor Felix.”
And he wrote the following letter:
Claudius Lysias, To His Excellency, Governor Felix: Greetings.
This man was seized by the Jews, and they were about to kill him when I came with my troops to rescue him. For I had learned that he is a Roman citizen,
and since I wanted to understand their charges against him, I brought him down to their Sanhedrin.
I found that the accusation involved questions about their own law, but there was no charge worthy of death or imprisonment.
When I was informed that there was a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once. I also instructed his accusers to present their case against him before you.
So the soldiers followed their orders and brought Paul by night to Antipatris.
The next day they returned to the barracks and let the horsemen go on with him.
When the horsemen arrived in Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.
The governor read the letter and asked what province Paul was from. Learning that he was from Cilicia,
he said, “I will hear your case when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that Paul be kept under guard in Herod’s Praetorium.

synedrion.gloss: The Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council of chief priests, elders, and scribes. The same body that condemned Jesus and Stephen now examines Paul, yet it cannot pronounce a lawful charge against him.

syneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner sense of right and wrong before God. Paul claims a good conscience to this day, not sinless perfection but integrity of conduct, and it is this claim that provokes the high priest to have him struck.

anastasis.gloss: Resurrection, a rising again of the dead. Paul names it as the true issue on trial; the risen Christ is the firstfruits of it, and the hope of resurrection is the ground of the Christian gospel that divides Pharisee from Sadducee.

martyreo.gloss: To bear witness, testify. The Lord tells Paul that as he has testified in Jerusalem, so he must testify in Rome; the same word runs through Acts, marking the church's task of witnessing to the risen Christ to the ends of the earth.

basic.context: Paul stands before the Sanhedrin, the council that had condemned Jesus. He claims a clear conscience and is struck on the mouth. Seeing the council split between Pharisees and Sadducees, he names the real issue, his hope in the resurrection, and the two parties turn on each other so fiercely that the Romans haul him out. That night the Lord promises Paul he will testify in Rome. When forty men vow to kill him, Paul's nephew exposes the plot, and the commander sends Paul under massive guard to Governor Felix.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, and its center is a single sentence, the Lord's promise in verse 11. Read the council scene, the plot, and the transfer to Caesarea in its light: everything that looks like danger is being bent toward one guaranteed end, Rome. Watch how God uses ordinary means, a divided council, a young nephew, a nervous commander, to keep His word.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul faces the synedrion, the Sanhedrin, and claims a good syneidesis, conscience. He redirects the trial to the anastasis, the resurrection, the true hope at stake. At the heart, the Lord tells him he must martyrein, bear witness, in Rome, the same witness word that drives all of Acts from Jerusalem outward.
basic.typology: Paul before the council that condemned his Lord walks in Christ's footsteps: struck, falsely handled, yet innocent. Like his Master, he commits himself to God rather than repaying evil. And as the Lord vindicated Jesus by raising Him, so He stands by Paul and pledges to bring him safely to his appointed testimony.
basic.christ: The risen Lord Himself appears at the turning point, standing by Paul in the night to say, Take courage. The hope Paul places on trial, the resurrection of the dead, is grounded in Christ, the firstfruits already raised. The living Jesus is not a distant idea but a present Lord who steadies His servant and guarantees his mission.
basic.god: God rules the whole tangle. He turns the council's own divisions to Paul's rescue, sends timely help through a boy, and moves a Roman officer to guard His witness with hundreds of soldiers. Above it all He speaks a promise and keeps it. Human plots and politics are no match for the purpose of God.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 22:30 sets up the council scene. Acts 9:15 promised Paul would witness before kings. 1 Corinthians 15:20 grounds resurrection hope in the risen Christ. Acts 27 and 28 record how God kept the promise of Rome through storm and shipwreck.
basic.oneStory: The gospel is moving from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and the empire's capital is the next great goal. Here, when councils rage and assassins swear, the risen Lord stands by His witness and names the destination: Rome. Every threat becomes a means, and the word cannot be stopped.

context.literary: The chapter continues the cycle of Paul's defenses as a prisoner and pivots the whole book toward Rome by the Lord's promise in verse 11. It sets the council scene, the assassination plot, and the transfer to Felix as a chain of dangers that God turns, one by one, into the road to the capital.
context.historical: The Sanhedrin united chief priests, elders, and scribes, divided between aristocratic Sadducees, who denied resurrection, angels, and spirits, and the Pharisees, who affirmed them. Ananias was a real and violent high priest of the period. A Roman commander could not lawfully hold a citizen against a murder plot without acting, which explains the extraordinary escort to Caesarea, the seat of the governor.
context.canonical: Verse 11 is a hinge of Acts, sealing the movement of the gospel to Rome that the book has been driving toward since chapter 1. The resurrection Paul places at the center of his trial ties the church's hope to the risen Christ and to the promise of a general resurrection at the last day.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke shows that no council, oath, or scheme can thwart God's purpose. By placing the Lord's promise of Rome at the center, he interprets every surrounding danger as a step God uses to keep His word and carry His witness to the empire's heart.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The council clash and the plot describe particular events, not a rulebook. The lasting truths are the centrality of the resurrection hope, the courage the risen Lord gives His servants, and God's sure providence over hostile powers.

debates.question: Was Paul right to divide the council by raising the resurrection in verse 6?

views.label: Yes, a shrewd and truthful move
views.case: The resurrection genuinely was the heart of the matter, for Paul preached the risen Christ, and he told no lie in calling himself a Pharisee who held that hope. The strongest case is that he wisely exposed the council's own bias and the emptiness of the case against him, and Luke records no rebuke; the Lord's encouragement follows that very night.

views.label: No, or at least questionable, a divisive tactic
views.case: Some judge that Paul provoked chaos rather than bore clear witness to Christ, using party rivalry to escape rather than to testify, and note that his sharp words to the high priest sit awkwardly beside his own quotation of the law. The strongest case is that the move looks more like self-defense than gospel proclamation.

debates.bearing: How we read verse 6 shapes how we think about wisdom under hostility: whether it is faithful to press the true point that divides truth from error, or whether we risk trading witness for mere tactical advantage; either way, resurrection remains the real issue Paul names.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on a few weighty words. Paul faces the synedrion, the council that had judged his Lord, and claims a good syneidesis, a clear conscience before God, integrity rather than sinlessness. He then names the anastasis, the resurrection, as the true matter on trial, splitting Pharisee from Sadducee. At the center stands the risen Lord's charge that Paul must martyrein, bear witness, in Rome, the same witness word that has propelled the church from Jerusalem outward since the opening of Acts. The vocabulary quietly insists that the trial is really about the risen Christ and the mission His resurrection sets in motion.

typology.type: The innocent one before the Sanhedrin
typology.fulfillment: Paul, struck and falsely handled before the council that condemned Jesus, follows the pattern of his Lord, who was struck and unjustly tried yet committed Himself to God, 1 Peter 2:23.
The resurrection Paul places on trial is secured in Christ, the firstfruits already raised, 1 Corinthians 15:20, guaranteeing the rising of all who are His.

typology.type: The Lord standing by His servant
typology.fulfillment: As God vindicated and raised Jesus, so the risen Lord stands by Paul with courage and a promise, a foretaste of the presence Christ gives His witnesses to the end of the age, Matthew 28:20.

study.christ: The risen Christ steps personally into the story at its hinge, standing by Paul in the night to command courage and to promise Rome. The hope Paul puts on trial, resurrection of the dead, is not an abstract doctrine but the reality secured in Jesus, the firstfruits already raised. Struck and falsely accused before the very council that condemned his Lord, Paul is conformed to Christ's own suffering, yet upheld by Christ's own presence. The living Jesus governs the trial, steadies His servant, and guarantees the witness will go on.
study.god: God's providence saturates the chapter. He turns the council's theological rift into Paul's deliverance, sends rescue through an unnamed nephew, and stirs a Roman commander to guard His witness with hundreds of troops through the night. Above every means stands His spoken promise, which no oath of forty men can break. The God who rules councils, plots, and empires keeps His word and brings His servant safely toward Rome.

crossReferences.note: The Lord's word that Paul would carry His name before kings and suffer for it, advanced here toward Rome.
Christ raised as the firstfruits grounds the resurrection hope Paul places at the center of his trial.
Christ, struck and unjustly tried, entrusted Himself to God, the pattern Paul follows before the council.
God renews the promise of Rome in the storm, showing His faithfulness to the word spoken here.

study.oneStory: The book of Acts is the story of the gospel going from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and Rome is the next great horizon. In this chapter, when the council rages and forty men swear to kill him, the risen Lord stands by His witness and names the destination: so also you must testify in Rome. Every danger becomes a means in God's hand, and the word presses on, unstoppable, toward the heart of the empire.

quiz.q: What did the Lord promise Paul the night after the council?

options.text: Take courage; as he had testified in Jerusalem, so he must testify in Rome.
options.note: This is the hinge of the chapter and of the book's movement toward Rome.

options.text: That he would be rescued from the plot against his life.
options.note: God did rescue him, but the promise specifically named his coming witness in Rome.

options.text: That the Sanhedrin would soon acquit him.
options.note: No acquittal is promised; the council remains hostile, and the promise concerns Rome.

options.text: That he would be released and return to his mission churches.
options.note: The promise pointed forward to Rome, not back to the mission field.

quiz.q: Why did Paul raise the resurrection before the council?

options.text: Because it was the true hope on trial, and it divided the Pharisees from the Sadducees.
options.note: Paul named the real issue, and it split the council between the two parties.

options.text: Because he wanted to set the council against itself and escape the trap.
options.note: It did divide them and relieve the pressure on him, but the resurrection was genuinely the heart of his gospel, not merely a tactic.

options.text: Because the Sadducees had asked him about life after death.
options.note: No such question is recorded; Paul himself introduced the resurrection.

options.text: Because he wished to deny that Jesus had risen.
options.note: The opposite: his hope in the resurrection rests on the risen Christ.

quiz.q: How was the plot of the forty men foiled?

options.text: Paul's nephew heard of it and reported it to the commander, who sent Paul away by night.
options.note: The young man exposed the ambush, and the commander acted to protect Paul.

options.text: The commander sent Paul to Caesarea under heavy guard.
options.note: True and part of the outcome, but the plot was uncovered first through Paul's nephew.

options.text: The conspirators broke their oath and gave up.
options.note: Nothing says they relented; their plan was thwarted by being exposed and by Paul's removal.

options.text: The Sanhedrin refused to cooperate with the plotters.
options.note: The plotters actually asked the council to help lure Paul; the council did not refuse on principle.

quiz.q: How does the chapter display God's providence?

options.text: God turns a divided council, a young nephew, and a Roman guard into means of keeping His promise of Rome.
options.note: Ordinary people and events all serve God's spoken word.

options.text: God rescues Paul from the assassination plot.
options.note: True, but it is one part of a wider providence ordering councils, family, and empire toward Rome.

options.text: God strikes down the high priest for his injustice.
options.note: Paul warns him, but no such judgment falls in the chapter; God's providence works through deliverance and guidance.

options.text: God leaves Paul to his own cleverness to survive.
options.note: The Lord Himself stands by Paul and directs events; Paul is not left to himself.

quiz.q: How did Paul respond after speaking harshly to the high priest?

options.text: He acknowledged Scripture's command not to speak evil of a ruler, saying he had not known he was high priest.
options.note: Paul submits to the written word and offers his explanation.

options.text: He apologized for losing his temper before the council.
options.note: He does step back and cite Scripture, but the text frames it as recognizing the ruler's office, not merely as an apology for temper.

options.text: He insisted the high priest deserved every word he said.
options.note: The reverse: Paul defers to the command not to speak evil of the ruler of the people.

options.text: He denied that he had said anything at all.
options.note: He does not deny his words; he explains them in light of Scripture.

questions: The Lord stood by Paul at his lowest point and said, Take courage. When have you sensed Christ's nearness in a hard season, and how did it steady you?
Paul put the resurrection at the center of everything. How central is the hope of resurrection to the way you actually live and face death?
God used an unnamed young nephew to save Paul's life. How does it encourage you that God works through ordinary, unseen people to accomplish His will?
Forty men swore an oath to stop Paul, yet God's promise held. Where are you tempted to think circumstances are stronger than God's word to you?
Paul was struck unjustly yet ultimately deferred to Scripture about honoring rulers. How do you respond when you are treated unfairly by those in authority?
The gospel was heading to Rome through chains and danger. Where might God be advancing His purposes in your life through circumstances that feel like setbacks?

translationNotes.note: Sanhedrin carries a footnoted alternative, the Council; the term recurs through the chapter for the same supreme Jewish body.
Paul quotes Exodus 22:28, do not speak evil about the ruler of your people, submitting his sharp words to the written law even under provocation.
The third hour of the night carries a footnoted explanation, that is, at nine tonight, marking the time of the secret night march to Caesarea.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 23. Footnote markers and line breaks were skipped when building verse text; the letter of Claudius Lysias in verses 26 was joined into one line with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
